title: TPP Friday Show - March 27, 2026
guest: Billy Bowman, Benjamin Miller, Faris Sbahi, Evan Loomis, Anvisha Pai, Ryan Tseng
transcript:
0:00
Breaking Down Novartis's $2B Acquisition of Allergy Drug Maker Excelergy

John (0:02): Today's Friday, March 27th, 2026 we are live from the TPP and Ultra realm
Jordy (0:06): Temple of Technology, the fortress of finance, the capital of capital.
John (0:10): Let me tell you about ramp.com. Time is money. Save both, easy, use corporate cards, go pay accounting and a whole lot more all in one place. Let's go. I like that one.
Jordy (0:23): Glad you like it sound 'cause it's the only one you have.
John (0:25): I have two. OK, let's be clear. I have this and I also have this, so be aware. What is that at the end? I heard some other music. Anyway, let's pull up the linear line up 'cause we have a great show for you today. Folks, It's Friday. We have Billy Bowman, his.
Jordy (0:40): They're calling him. They're calling him the Bominator. The Bominator.
John (0:44): If you're in LA, if you're in Hollywood and you drive down the freeway, you will see as you enter Hollywood his name as big as the Hollywood sign. Basically, there's a mass.
Jordy (0:55): Fiver paid for it.
John (0:57): And so we're gonna have him in the studio talking about what it takes to be a director of AI video, what that even means, how he's using these tools, what he's using. That'll be interesting. Then we have Benjamin Miller from Fundrise talking about taking private stakes in private companies, public on the public Stock Exchange, right?
Jordy (1:16): Yeah, it's up 1300% above NAV.
John (1:22): And then we have a lending round and we're closing it out with Ryan from Shield AI, who just raised a massive round. Defence tech is of course booming and we will talk to him a lot about what AI means in the defence stack. Linear of course is the system for modern software development. 70% of enterprise workspaces, 75% now of workspaces on Linear are using agents and you should be too.
John (1:45): So why is no one talking about Excelergy? Love these titles. Very niche company. Was in stealth mode for five years but sold this week to Novartis for 2 billion smackeroos. That's a good outcome.
John (2:01): Novartis of course is the $300 billion Swiss pharmaceutical company and they just paid $2 billion for basically a 5 year old company named Excelergy. Exactly. It's a great outcome. It's a fantastic outcome. This is an interesting story because it's very much like the biotech playbook.
John (2:17): Done right, they bring in the right CEO.
Jordy (2:20): Tyler is through this $2 billion exit in five years. From your point of view, is this good?
John (2:26): Is this good?
Tyler (2:28): That's pretty good, yeah.
John (2:30): I agree it's, but this isn't in the sweet spot for typical tech news, but there are some interesting data points. Amir over at The Information said leading indicator of AI adoption drug companies like Novo Nordisk Pharma has long been at the forefront of machine learning.
John (2:50): Ozempic maker says AI agents are shortening its clinical trials. People think that it's going to a prompt and saying like cure cancer. It's not. It's like you have a 10,000 page document that you need to send to the FDA and if there's a comma in the wrong place, they can just send it back to you and say there's an error like we don't want to deal with it right now.
Jordy (3:12): Is that real?
John (3:13): You can get dinged for crazy stuff like I don't know exactly what I can share, but I've heard of multi billion dollar companies getting dinged for not showing like they will list out OK, we use this centrifuge to run this assay to understand how this chemical separates. But they didn't list out who the suppliers to that supplier are. They didn't explain the entire supply chain and there was like very little guidance on it. Like little things like that come up all the time and reading through all the documentation. Of course, most of this when you're talking about Novo, it's like they have Direct Line of communication with the FDA. So it's different but.
Jordy (3:54): Seeing this headline.
John (3:54): There are tons of situations where we need to comb through data and organize.
Jordy (3:58): When some type of public company software CEO comes out and says like everything is accelerating because of AI. Often times I look at the business and if revenue isn't accelerating dramatically, you think like, OK, this guy just wants an AI narrative. He's just reaching for an AI narrative here. But when I see a pharma company do it, no real context on pharma personally. So I'm like, it must be true.
John (4:26): No, I mean, I was working on FDA filings like four or five years ago and there were so many Word documents that needed to be part. It was the same application for multiple variations on the product that the FDA was going to review. And so we actually wrote Python scripts to programmatically interact with the Word documents to basically do like a very advanced version of find and replace. It's like perfect for AI and it just speeds things up a little bit. You submit a week earlier, you have, you can submit more things, more variations, you can give more information, compile more information, investigate other literature, do deep research reports. What else is out there? Let's review all the IP faster, summarize things, find different threads to pull on. Little things like that just squeak out like an extra 5 minutes here, an extra day there, and it all adds up to acceleration in progress. It's not like an overnight, all of a sudden everything's completely changed. Maybe that happens. But at least right now there's clearly a benefit.
John (5:21): But this was an overnight success, at least for Alex Eagle, Ted Jardinski, Luke Pennington and Jeff Harris, who took academic research from labs at Stanford and Byrne into what is basically a VC backed biotech company. So the goal was simple. They wanted to make a better allergy drug. So when people have allergies, if peanuts come in, your allergy cells flare up and you need to fight that back. So the current blockbuster drug in the category is called Zolair. And when someone has an allergic reaction to a food allergy, drugs act quickly to treat the reaction before it does lasting damage. So the key immune molecule at the center of many allergic reactions is called IgE, and Excelergy's lead program, EXL 111, targets IgE. So the goal is to work faster, quiet the pathway more deeply, and ideally last longer between doses.
John (6:33): So Red Tree Venture Capital seeded the company in 2021, but they stayed in stealth for about 5 years. The team announced their first big fundraise, a $70 million Series A led by Samsara Biocapital. And this is a pretty typical flow for biotech companies. University science first, then they bring on secondary specialist capital. It's less common to go to the big names like the Sequoias, the Founders Funds. They do some stuff in bio, but very often if it's coming directly out of a lab, going to be IPO'd or acquired in a few years. That's a pretty traditional biotech VC playbook.
John (7:15): And so by February of this year, Excelergy dosed its first phase one patients. So they gave the drug to subjects and it was obviously looking good because the Novartis deal signed a few weeks later.
Jordy (7:21): How well does that phase one trial need to go for somebody to immediately come in and be like, Yep, here's 2 billion?
John (7:28): Probably really well. But it is like standing on the shoulders of giants. The research was done in 2020-2021, probably earlier and it's building on an existing pathway.
Jordy (7:44): This CEO is such a chad.
John (7:47): Yeah insane. So I didn't want to diminish his accomplishments by reducing it to a playbook. But this isn't his first rodeo. The CEO, Todd Zovodnik, joined in 2025, sells the company in less than a year. And you've seen the story twice before when he was at Celtic, Allergan acquired it for 2.4 billion. And when he ran Dermavant, he sold that company for 1.2 billion in 2024. So he's seen a Unicorn outcome 2 years ago and he gets a new job, gets back in the arena and immediately sells the company. So clearly a great operator.
Jordy (8:32): Join his next company. That's a good idea.
John (8:36): Just follow this guy around.
Jordy (8:38): $3 billion acquisitions back-to-back.
John (8:45): I would love to be a janitor here. I'll take all stock. I'll take all stock and I will clean the toilets and get coffee if that's what it takes, because I know what's going to happen. I've seen this playbook before. Anyway, Novartis stock is not really moving on this news because this is very expected. This is part of their playbook. Last year, 2025, they made $1.7 billion in sales of Zolair. And this is a very logical next act in that category. Key Zolair patents are about to expire. So a next generation IgE asset is strategically valuable right now. Novartis CEO has previously outlined a broad acquisition playbook focused on bolt on deals in core areas. So when they have a business up and running like immunology, they have all of the sales and distribution, they have an existing product portfolio. The business is working, add things on to extend the viability of that business line and Excelergy fits squarely inside of that plan.
John (9:53): Now the $2 billion acquisition price, it's not all cash upfront. It is split between an upfront payment and milestones based on development and commercial targets. So if they flop in phase two trials, probably some of that gets clawed back. But they have Novartis behind them to propel them through that. And it feels like a pretty standard playbook. So good stuff there. Anyone who's dealt with allergies, it's always a hassle. Anything that can treat that is obviously good. So another arrow in the quiver of modern medicine.

10:45
Unpacking Anthropic's New Mythos Model and Pentagon Legal Battle

Jordy (11:05): Breaking news. Huge leak. OMG, did you guys know that Anthropic was training a better model this whole time? This is shocking.
John (11:23): I wonder if this will trigger DeepMind or OpenAI to consider training a better model. You could see multiple companies training new models based on this leak.
John (11:40): There's some interesting marketing perspective and how you actually roll out and manage vibes.
Jordy (11:46): Yeah, to me, it doesn't seem improbable that there's been a lot of hype talk on the timeline this week.
John (11:54): You got to start pumping Mythos.
Jordy (11:56): Mythos talk.
John (11:57): The most powerful AI model ever developed. It's a good tag.
Jordy (12:00): And very painful for all the SaaS companies out there that just sell off whenever Anthropic does something.
John (12:11): Prins wrote a little summary. Anthropic has been testing a new model called Mythos with certain customers. They call it a step change in AI capabilities, including dramatically higher scores in coding, academic reasoning, and cybersecurity. I want to know how it will do on ARC AGI V3, because all of the models are under 1% completion on that benchmark. It's the one benchmark that is not saturated.
John (12:49): So this is part of a new Capybara series of models, which are larger and more intelligent than Opus. It's more expensive to run and it's not ready yet for general release, but they are excited about it. Mythos pricing bets coming in from .005 seconds $100 in 250 out. Subs reprice to 50 dollars 250 and a $1000 plan might be coming.
Tyler (15:16): I think just back on the pricing thing, this is generally right. Basically it's saying it's just going to get 10X more expensive because right now I think it's something like $10 in, $25 out.
John (15:28): People are ready to pay more though, they're paying a bunch through the API, they're paying more for Cursor.
Tyler (15:33): Maybe we'll talk about this right after. But the subscription, technically it's the same price, but the limits are going down. So the tokens are getting more expensive. And as you see, there's this insane compute crunch, I assume that models are going to get much more expensive as they're getting bigger.
John (15:48): Yeah, it is interesting. I wasn't expecting to be in the regime of subscriptions as long as we have been. Ben on our team sort of bounces around from one subscription to the next. He'll max one out and then move over to the next one. He's maybe more price sensitive than model sensitive.
Tyler (16:20): So the reason I'm not hitting the APIs is because it is like basically 10X more expensive than using the subscription.
John (16:26): The subscription, so you do most of the work in the subscription, which is economically rational. I'm surprised by how many people who are in a business context still make that decision and how much of a price war we're in.
John (17:54): Takim has an independent take. He's the biggest NVIDIA bull and he's making the argument that Anthropic needs more GPUs. To manage growing demand for Claude, we're adjusting our five hour session limits for free pro Max Subs during peak hours.
Jordy (18:16): And Takim is coming on the show Monday.
John (18:23): And so a lot of people are getting into polyphasic sleep because of this. So Claude has different limits at different times. So when the limits are in effect, that's when you need to be sleeping.
Tyler (18:38): I'm not there yet, but the problem is getting worse.
John (18:42): I told Tyler sometimes I indulge in polyphasic sleep after the show, I'll sleep for an hour and then go about my day and then sleep at night. And he said that's just a nap. It doesn't count. People really are doing the polyphasic sleep thing to monitor their agents.
Tyler (19:16): If you're trying to get around the limits, polyphasic sleep doesn't make sense. You just need to basically be nocturnal. You need to be inverse what San Francisco hours are.
John (19:33): It seems like it's much better to just have a prompt desk that is geographically distributed evenly across the globe. So when your American employee stops the prompting, goes home for the night, then your European colleague steps up and starts prompting on the same project, and then your colleague in Asia can pick up when Europe goes to bed. So you have 24/7 coverage.
John (20:13): There's actually two things going on. One where you're waiting for your agents to respond and if it's going to take hours for a prompt to return, you should go take a nap because you're better off checking in on a prompt every two hours for the whole day for 24 hours. That's separate from trying to get around rate limits. Yes, everyone agrees more GPUs more CPUs more chips. We continue to be chip constrained even Anthropic. It appears to be chip constrained despite incredible fundraising and partnerships with hyperscalers. It really is a vindication of everyone who is saying AI is not a bubble and that the models will get better.
John (21:44): I mean, last March, a year ago, people were like 50 billion dollars, 100 billion dollars. Is this stuff that useful? Is it slop? But what's the real economic value? Certainly it can't be more than 20 bucks a month or $200 a month. And now lots of people are maybe only paying a few thousand a month because they're using a bunch of subscriptions. But they're pretty close to justifying the Jensen $250K a year per engineer thing.
Jordy (22:43): I think the big thing is there's going to be more use cases that have a similar level of product market fit as code gen that use tokens on the level that code gen tools do. And we haven't fully identified those yet.
John (23:36): There are some people that would need to make 500 financial models a day like Roe Kana makes hundreds of trades every single day. If you're doing a proper full DCF pulling every SEC filing and doing that hundreds of times a day because you're trading, you want real time models sort of like rebuilding a Bloomberg Terminal for yourself.
John (24:13): Anyway, let's move over to the preliminary injunction. Anthropic has been granted a preliminary injunction re the Pentagon supply chain risk designation in California but is allowing stay for one week. I think a lot of people, the tech community broadly came away with this prediction that this wouldn't hold, that the supply chain thing wasn't really going to stick.
John (24:59): Dean Ball says this is a devastating ruling for the government, finding Anthropic likely to prevail on essentially all of its theories for why the government's actions were unlawful and unconstitutional. One of the things she mentions is the huge range of amici briefs supporting Anthropic.
John (25:32): We found my limit. On a personal note, says Dean Ball, some friends and allies on the right who have been angry at me for my own words and actions. Anyone who thinks I spoke out for personal gain or trivial reasons against an administration I served in is crazy. Dean Ball served in the second Trump administration. He wrote the AI policy thing. He worked on that.
Jordy (25:59): It's the plural of amicus.
John (26:01): This is brutal for me because I studied Latin and I should be able to nail this. Anyway, it was a hugely costly decision for Dean Ball, but this judge's ruling shows why I did it. This is a staggeringly illegal act by the government. That is why I'm particularly honored to have been implicitly quoted in the ruling for calling this what it was when Secretary Hegseth made his initial announcement, an attempted act of corporate murder. This case continues but Anthropic has scored a very large win here.
John (27:04): The other scoop is from Axios. Sam Altman apparently told staff he tried to save Anthropic in the Pentagon Clash.
Jordy (27:14): Which he apparently thought was somewhat ironic given that said competitor openly blasted him on any podcasts that they go on.
John (27:30): OK. Well, last Anthropic story, IPO, they're trying to go public as early as Q4 with a potential 60 billion raise. They're racing. And Anthropic is up at 70% on Kalshi for IPO this year. They jumped up from 46% to over 70%. Jersey Mike's still beating them both at 75%. SpaceX at 94%.
Jordy (28:06): Discord is also sitting at 63%.
John (28:10): Discord what a crazy story. I love Jason Citron so much, the founder, but he is out I think.

28:37
Exploring JFK's Former Penthouse and Luxury Home Ski Bars

Jordy (28:42): OpenAI has surpassed 100 million in annualized ad run rate. Ad pilot launched 6 weeks ago. It's expanded to 600 advertisers and plans to launch self-serve advertiser access in April.
John (29:07): The first ad for the Wall Street Journal is a very good omen. New ad platforms often deliver really good results before people figure them out. Big companies move slower than you. If you can be the first person to advertise, figure it out.
Jordy (29:47): What's this news? Apple's talking about opening up Siri to rival AI services.
John (29:54): Weird.
Jordy (30:00): Guess who wrote this story?
John (30:01): I'm going to guess the Germinator.
Jordy (30:03): That's right. Apple plans to open Siri to outside AI assistance. The company is developing new tools to allow AI chatbots installed via the App Store to integrate with the Siri assistant, enabling users to send queries to services. The change is part of an attempt to turn around Apple's fortunes in AI and could allow Apple to generate more money from third party AI subscriptions through the App Store.
John (31:20): I think there's also the antitrust angle. They have had to allow you to change the default search engine in Safari. You can actually reroute that to ChatGPT. But I have wanted for a long time to reroute the Siri button to just ChatGPT, instant, fast, ultra fast. But every time I do it, it loads and it says working with ChatGPT. And then it takes a little bit too long. You're always fighting in the UI between Apple Intelligence or Siri or ChatGPT. So we will see where this goes.
John (33:14): So Barry Diller just paid $11 million for JFK's favorite New York penthouse. An entity linked to the billionaire purchased Karen Pritzker's Co-op at the Carlisle, the same unit where Kennedy famously stayed during his visits to the city in the 50s and 60s. John F Kennedy stayed in a duplex penthouse at the Carlisle Hotel so often that it became known as the New York White House. After his assassination, his widow and two children lived in the Manhattan Hotel for about 10 months.
John (34:16): Now an entity linked to billionaire Barry Diller has purchased Kennedy's favorite penthouse for $11 million. Diller bought the Co-op unit from film producer Karen Pritzker and heiress to the Hyatt Hotel fortune who bought the apartment for 12 million in 2007. Interesting that the high end penthouse market has not moved that much.
John (35:51): Speaking of wet bars, forget apres ski because homeowners are now building their own bars in custom spaces where they can relax and get toasty after a day on the slopes. For Brian Healy, what comes after a day on the mountain sometimes takes precedence over the skiing itself. When he built his Lake Tahoe area vacation home in 2019, he spent about $800,000 to build a bar area on the main floor of the house.
Jordy (36:14): He can ski to from the slopes. North Star. That's bad. Sounds great.
John (36:24): He said I've joked that I designed a bar and built a house around it. This is my dream. I'm going to build a movie theater and then build a house around it.
John (37:17): Healy, 51, is an Ireland native. He runs an energy company in the San Francisco Bay Area. After a day on the slopes, Healy and his pals head to the space dubbed Healy's Bar, leaving their gear in the storage area of the Truckee, CA, property. And sometimes other skiers end up at Healy's home after getting lost while skiing through the trees. It's an open door policy, he says. We invite them in for beer.
John (38:29): But instead of heading to a crowded bar for cocktails, more and more wealthy homeowners are hosting friends at private bar areas with high end mountain inspired finishes. These bars have features like specialty ice makers, dishwashers, decorative lighting and dramatic finishes.
John (39:31): Bruce Johnson, a retired lumber executive, and his wife, Wellness coach Peggy Duffy. They wanted a Mountain inspired aesthetic for the bar in their Edwards CO home, which is about 10 minutes from Beaver Creek Resort. Their daughter-in-law, interior designer Jackie Johnson, worked on the $200,000 space, which has open wine storage columns and sconces wrapped in brown suede.
John (40:59): Jackie suggests using winter forward fabrics such as alpaca fur, heron hides and mohair. In designing his bar, Healey looked to his Irish heritage for inspiration using green leather bar stools, a green marble countertop and vintage green glassware.
John (42:04): Developer Robert Covington put 80s video games like Pac-Man next to the lower level apres ski bar of a home he recently built in Steamboat Springs, CO. The roughly 7400 square foot home asking 13 million is located near the base of Mount Werner.
John (42:36): For some homeowners, apres ski entertaining is more of a state of mind. Jewelry designer Jane Berg, 64, and her husband, marketing executive Kevin Berg, 70, built a house next to the slopes of Colorado Aspen Highlands.

44:47
From Fenway Gyms to Drew Barrymore's Quick Home Sale

Jordy (44:47): A retired pitcher's home with a gym inspired by Fenway asks 23 million. James Shields was a star pitcher in the major leagues for 13 seasons. So when he and his wife decided to build a California home in 2018, they were amused to discover that the lot they selected was shaped like a baseball diamond. They bought the Rancho Santa Fe property the next day for 1.8 million. The pair spent the next few years designing a roughly 13,500 square foot house with a gym, pool, putting green and bowling alley.
John (45:29): Bowling alley is underrated. I know some of you folks have seen There Will Be Blood, bad things can happen in bowling alleys, but great things can happen in bowling alleys.
Jordy (46:02): What kind of rig are they using in the bowling alley? Because we learned earlier this week that maintaining these bowling alley machines...
John (46:11): You can't win. If you go with the good machines, they're from the 50s, it's going to be a nightmare to maintain.
John (48:05): The gym has a brick wall to evoke Fenway Park, where Shields took a swing at Coco Crisp in 2008.
John (48:40): So in December, Lauren Toronto, a real estate agent at Coldwell Banker was at a $12.75 million listing in Naples, FL. It was a 5 bedroom neoclassical home. This house has a castle like feel with Rotunda ceilings, turrets in the rear. It also has a curved mahogany staircase that serves as a focal point. This real estate agent calls it the wedding staircase. She'd been at the property for an open house, but it started to rain. As she was outside taking the signs down, she sees a couple in wedding attire on the beach trying to take photos. She invited them to finish up inside and get out of the rain. They climbed the staircase and took a few more photos. The couple were adorable, in their 20s. I didn't have any champagne in the refrigerator, so we just toasted with water in plastic cups. It was a darling and magic moment.
John (50:42): Drew Barrymore put a Westchester home on the market two years after buying it. She was fed up with her long drive to the Hamptons. Barrymore said she felt a karmic connection to the Harrison NY property. She lives in Manhattan with her two daughters. She bought the roughly 12 acre estate for 4.4 million, did an extensive renovation, but roughly 2 years later is listing the property for 4.99 million.
Jordy (52:35): So she's flipping it at a loss.
John (52:37): The renovation took longer than expected. The estate includes a roughly 5600 square foot five bedroom main house, a pool, a pool house. Barrymore said she previously owned a home in Sagaponack, but the distance and the weekend traffic to the Hamptons became untenable. When she purchased the home, she thought it would only need a cosmetic renovation. Instead, it turned into a complete internal gut.

54:26
Billy Bowman's Journey from Fashion to Leading AI Visual Creation

John (54:32): Billy Bowman, good to meet you. How you doing?
Billy Bowman (54:34): I'm really good.
John (54:37): What is your life like right now? Are you just getting blown up by everyone?
Billy Bowman (54:42): Probably, yes, I haven't even had time to check all the DMs yet. I'm really doing my best just to take care of everything.
Jordy (54:47): We were live on the show reading about this whole thing because we had seen the ad on the 101.
John (54:55): I saw it in person. I drove right by it.
Jordy (55:10): So for anybody that missed it, there is a massive Hollywood sign scale...
Billy Bowman (55:16): Yeah, 30 feet tall, 230 feet wide.
Jordy (55:21): And on a hillside that I haven't seen any out of home advertising on before. How did this happen? Were you on Fiverr very active?
Billy Bowman (55:37): So actually, no, but the creative director Nir Rufua reached out to me. And I thought first of all, you're joking. He was not joking. Then secondly, it's just a billboard, right? No, no, no. We're building the sign. It's been the work since October last year.
John (56:28): Take us back to the beginning. What did you study in school?
Billy Bowman (56:46): How the hell did I end up here? Creativity is the only thing I'm good at. I grew up in a creative family, my mom and dad ran a small advertising agency my entire life. My mom was the graphic designer and dad was the art director. I'm classically trained. I started at Beckman's College of Design in Stockholm. Ended up going into fashion, worst decision of my life. I worked at All Saints, H&M, bunch of different brands.
Jordy (57:57): Why was it the worst decision?
Billy Bowman (58:00): You've seen Devil Wears Prada, right? That's how it actually is. It's constant, you should be happy to be here, in the worst toxic way. No career prospects. You're basically licking ass for 10 years and maybe you'll be the creative director.
Billy Bowman (58:24): So eventually I pivoted to tech in 2018-2019. I worked at PayPal in Sweden and Vodafone, bunch of different brands until I ended up in an AI startup in 2023. This was a tinkering with Midjourney since mid 2022. That very first one came out and I'm like, I can actually get like a hovering sneaker in the air and it looks like something.
Billy Bowman (59:13): So from there we did a bunch of stuff over 2023 with a German AI startup. At the tail end of 2023, I've been posting some spec ads that were maybe a couple of levels up what most people were seeing. And all of a sudden my DMs are people from the first AI agencies, monks, the big agencies.
John (59:46): Explain the workflow for those first ads. Is that static images that are AI generated, then text layout in Figma? Or is it video from start to finish?
Billy Bowman (1:00:05): Back then, it's like just getting anything that felt realistic, had a bit of grit and texture to it. Correct prompting. Just having an eye for art direction, angles, lighting. And editing. Where to put the logo and getting good pacing so it feels like an ad.
Billy Bowman (1:00:42): So back to 2024, did a bunch of work, did a music video for Hardy and Fred Durst. Then 2025, we did the activation campaign for Taylor Swift. We worked with Luis Capaldi, with Google and YouTube. Universal Music Group. A lot of really big brands together with Wonder Studios.
Jordy (1:02:58): What is your team look like to execute on all this?
Billy Bowman (1:03:01): It's growing, but the biggest bottleneck is finding people. I also teach at Sweden's biggest creative school called Berg's School of Communication. They have what we think the world's only AI program at this point. One year in classroom, government funded. And I'm there for three weeks. The ones with a freelance appetite tend to come to me.
Jordy (1:03:39): On the projects in 2025, what does a team look like? How quickly are you completing them?
Billy Bowman (1:03:59): Having a lean and mean squad is better. We have to have a really strong lead and then delegate. A lean squad of four or five. For Luis Capaldi, we were seven artists total working under the Wonder Studios umbrella.
Jordy (1:06:13): How has your stack evolved? What models were you using in 2024-2025?
Billy Bowman (1:06:26): Up until 24, Midjourney was king and queen. Then you had Flux and other more indie ones. Then Google entered the chat really big with VO3. The Google suite has become kind of industry standard. And now Kling 3 and Seedance 2. They're king on the hill now in AI video.
Jordy (1:07:08): Over VO3?
Billy Bowman (1:07:09): Yeah, as it stands right now, yes.
John (1:07:11): How is your access to Seedance?
Billy Bowman (1:07:23): I was fortunate enough to be in the Dreamina CPP, creative partnership program. I've had pre access for a month or so.
Jordy (1:07:48): So why is their model better? Because they just downloaded every Hollywood film?
Billy Bowman (1:07:55): We can only speculate. The data set and the training is, you know...
Jordy (1:08:01): Just basically higher value data than even YouTube? Because YouTube is like...
Billy Bowman (1:08:05): Potentially, yeah.
Jordy (1:08:28): So how has prompt engineering evolved?
Billy Bowman (1:08:57): Prompt engineering was a thing and synthography and all these titles. People tend to want to make things more fancy than what they are because they want to hold on to the old idea that getting good output is technically difficult.
John (1:09:15): And that's just gone. That was the case with being a master of After Effects or Photoshop. So even if your ideas are mediocre, you're the only person that can actually instantiate anything.
Billy Bowman (1:09:32): Used to have this technical moat. But prompt engineering has changed a lot in the sense that the models have gotten so much better understanding natural language.
Jordy (1:10:56): Do you think people are good at spotting deep fakes today?
Billy Bowman (1:11:31): Runway put out a pretty funny AB test. They used real start frames and then one was just continued and the other was animated with AI. Anyone can go and take that test right now.
John (1:11:50): The New York Times ran a similar thing with poetry, literature, science fiction, and a lot of the readers preferred the AI generated content.
Jordy (1:12:03): What industries are hitting you up? Is there anyone saying we're never going to do this?
Billy Bowman (1:12:59): For us, it's been brand and content and commercials mostly because the inns we have with agencies is how we position ourselves.
Jordy (1:13:50): What advantages are companies getting? Is it speed? Cost? Flexibility?
Billy Bowman (1:13:50): Don't spend less on budget. Spend more on imagination. If everything is about cost reduction, in this media landscape with everything going on, ads are going to look better than ever. That rising tide lifts all boats. So that's a completely backward strategy.
John (1:14:23): If you're a brand spending $1 million on creative assets and you say great, AI is here, I'm only going to spend 100K. Well, your competitor is going to spend the same million, but they're going to get 10 times, maybe 100 times as much content and they're going to smoke you.
Billy Bowman (1:14:41): Or even just the nature of the content. Because we can put the camera on an arrow flying through a battlefield and over the monkey and another squirrel takes it. Anything.
Billy Bowman (1:15:06): We did a protein bar ad. The brief was fly through, down through the clouds, the girl is glamping in Argentina and she eats a bar and then feeds it to a capybara that starts twerking.
Jordy (1:15:27): You know capybaras can get violent.
Billy Bowman (1:15:44): And there you have the sustainability issue as well, the carbon footprint of flying out to do all this the traditional way.
Jordy (1:16:28): Somebody making a film would fly out fifty people to another continent to get scenes that would ultimately take maybe 60 seconds.
Jordy (1:16:48): What advice are you giving to creatives?
Billy Bowman (1:17:31): I typically say it's not about AI or, it's AI and. We did a Super Bowl commercial for a big airline, all the talent were shot green screen, we background replaced and relit them. So you don't even have to choose anymore. Just test it. Don't say anything until you've tested it.
Jordy (1:18:44): But still, that is dramatically more than just typing into the box what you want.
Billy Bowman (1:18:50): Oh yeah, because you have to think about casting, demographics, everything.
Jordy (1:19:02): What feedback do you give the various labs on what they're doing badly?
Billy Bowman (1:19:23): What we're doing is what I call live art directed realism. If it doesn't look like a movie frame, it's not good enough for us. What I want is better, more human focused interfaces.
John (1:20:12): Have you thought about getting into vibe coding, building the tools that you want?
Billy Bowman (1:20:21): There's just no time. The exhaustion of keeping up is insane.
Jordy (1:20:43): How many Super Bowl ads do you think you'll do for next Super Bowl?
Billy Bowman (1:20:46): We'll see.
John (1:21:35): What is the integration with Fiverr actually look like?
Billy Bowman (1:21:59): There's a lot more high budget stuff on Fiverr. What we're doing with the hub is they've curated a solid selection of some of the best. It's hard to find vetted industry pros because there's like a handful of people in the world that do this at a level good enough for commercial production.
Jordy (1:22:36): At any given point, there's only maybe like 100 people in the world that actually do great leading edge work. Do you think that will stay the same with AI?
Billy Bowman (1:23:31): Absolutely. The floor is raised, but so is the ceiling. Standing out is also more difficult.
Jordy (1:24:21): How are you building the company?
Billy Bowman (1:24:33): Step by step, brick by brick. It's literally been me and my co-founder for the last years doing this.
